title: What I have been Working on in RStudio
text:
Recreating an Existing Figure
This code chunk shows how I rebuilt a figure from the original project using only the published plot and the underlying dataset as references. I didn’t have access to the original code, so I recreated the visualization by matching its structure, aesthetics, and variables through careful inspection.

library(tidyverse) # reading in the tidyverse
library(janitor) # reading janitor package 
library(dplyr) # reading in dyplr package 
library(rstatix) # reading in rstatix package
library(flextable) # reading in flextable package 
library(lubridate) # reading in the lubridate package 

temp <- read_csv("temp_2024.csv") # Reading in data for problem 4 as an object called temp


temp_clean <- temp |>
  
 
  clean_names() |>
  
 
  mutate(year = year(date_local)) |>
  
  
  filter(site %in% c("NAPL", "SCDI", "IVEE") &
           year == "2024") |>
  
  
  mutate(site = case_when(
    site == "IVEE" ~ "Isla Vista",
    site == "NAPL" ~ "Naples",
    site == "SCDI" ~ "Santa Cruz - Diablo"
  )) |>
  

  group_by(site, date_local) |> 
  summarize(mean_daily = mean(temp_c)) |> 
  ungroup() 

`summarise()` has regrouped the output.
ℹ Summaries were computed grouped by site and date_local.
ℹ Output is grouped by site.
ℹ Use `summarise(.groups = "drop_last")` to silence this message.
ℹ Use `summarise(.by = c(site, date_local))` for per-operation grouping
  (`?dplyr::dplyr_by`) instead.



library(ggplot2)

ggplot(temp_clean, aes(x = date_local,  # x axis using adte local data 
                       y = mean_daily, # y axis using mean daily data
                       color = site)) + # making different colors for each site 
  geom_line() +  # only one geometry which is a line
  labs(
    x = "Date", # labeling x and y axis 
    y = "Mean daily temperature (°C)", # labeling x and y axis
    title = "Reef Bottom Tempratures at Isla Vista, Naples, Santa Cruz Island", # Adding a subtitle to describe categories
    color = "Site", #  using different colors for each site 
    caption = "Data source : Santa Barbara Coastal LTER" # adding a caption to the bottom to show where the data was sourced 
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c( # changing the colors from the default 
    "Isla Vista" = "red", # making "Isla Vista" red 
    "Naples" = "yellow", # making "Naples" yellow 
    "Santa Cruz - Diablo" = "green" # making "Santa Cruz" green 
  )) +
  scale_x_date(
               date_labels = "%b %y")+ # format as June 24, Mar 24 etc. 
  theme_minimal() +  # Changing the theme form the default 
  theme( 
    legend.position = c(0.2, 0.8), # moving the legend  to the position it should be in 
    plot.title.position = "plot",  # left-align title cause I thought it looked better 
    panel.grid.major = element_blank(), # removing major gridline 
    panel.grid.minor  = element_blank(),# removing minor gridline 
    panel.border = element_rect(), # adding the plain black border
    axis.ticks = (element_line(color = "black")) # adding ticks to the x and y axis 
  )

Taking field soil samples to evaluate whether we can use plant traits to map soil mineralogy from remotely sensed imagery.










No matching items

title: Serpentine Soil Sampling
text:
Serpentine Soils & Plant Indicators
Serpentine soils are some of the harshest environments in California. They are low in essential nutrients, high in heavy metals, and notoriously difficult for plants to thrive in. At Sedgwick Reserve, these extreme soils sit right beside more typical sedimentary units, creating a perfect natural experiment for studying how geology shapes plant communities.


Can Plants Reveal What’s Beneath the Surface?
During NASA’s SHIFT campaign, researchers measured plant traits across these contrasting geologic units while AVIRIS‑NG collected weekly airborne imagery. Because plants growing on serpentinite often show distinct physiological signatures, their leaves may act as indicators of the soil chemistry below. Building on this work, I’m collecting soil samples from existing SHIFT plots to measure elemental concentrations and directly link them to foliar chemistry. By connecting geology → soils → plant traits, this project tests whether remotely sensed plant trait maps can be used to infer subsurface soil and geochemical properties


Some fun Field Photos

title: My Mosaic Art
text:
I enjoy making mosaic art. Over the years, I’ve experimented with different materials, colors, and patterns, and each project has taught me something new about patience, texture, and design. Here are a few of my past pieces, each one with its own little story and creative process behind it. All materials come form thrifted and recycled glassware that I shape using a scoring and glass cutting method.


This mosaic depicts a couple based on a reference image. I loved interpreting the connection through my own style and the way I arranged each piece of glass. Creating it was a rewarding process, but giving it as a gift and seeing how much it meant was the best part.




I created a mosaic design on this bedframe, using color and pattern to turn a simple piece of furniture into something expressive and personal. Waking up and seeing it every morning makes me genuinely happy because it’s a reminder of the time and care I put into it.




For my fist ever experience working with glass mosaics, I covered this pot, experimenting with texture and shape to give it a unique, decorative surface. Though this project I learned how to work on curved surfaces and how to use a glass cutter/nipper.

title: Some Cool Hikes
text:
Why I love hiking
I love getting outside and hiking whenever I have free time . It’s my favorite way to reset and clear my mind. Being on a trail always reminds me how good it feels to slow down and be surrounded by nature. Here are some of my favorites!


Mount Langley
My mom and I originally planned to take three days (2 nights) to hike Mount Langley, but once we were out there, we ended up speeding up and down the mountain in just 2 days. It’s one of my favorite memories from laughing at how fast the plan changed, to feeling proud of what we pulled off.



Half Dome
We started our Half Dome hike at 3 a.m., moving through the dark with headlamps and that mix of nerves and excitement you only get on big adventures. The cables were the most fun part for me, and it was amazing watching people in our group face their fears and push themselves all the way to the top.



Mount Baldy
This one was only meant to be a training hike for mount Langley but it ended up being one of my favorites. We started the day at the base of the hike in Los Angeles hiked 9 miles and got to take the ski lifts down the rest of the hike.

title: Monitoring HABs With SCCOOS
text: The Southern California Coastal Ocean Observing System (SCCOOS) is a regional network that monitors the health of our coastal ocean. They collect data on currents, water quality, plankton, HABs, and more to help scientists and the public understand what’s happening along our coastline in real time. I am a research assistant for SCCOOS!

title: Monitoring HABs With SCCOOS
section: Me counting Santa Barbara coastal HABS
text: Me counting Santa Barbara coastal HABS

title: Kyra Baranick
text:
Hi, welcome to my site.
I’m Kyra Baranick, a Hydrologic Sciences and Policy student at UC Santa Barbara who loves digging into how water moves through landscapes and how people interact with it. I split my time between coding in R, studying water movement, and getting muddy in the field collecting soil, seawater, and phytoplankton samples.
I’m especially interested in groundwater, flood behavior, and the big, messy questions around water supply in California.
When I’m not working on research or class projects, you’ll usually find me exploring California’s water infrastructure, hiking around the coast, or hanging out with friends. This site is a place to share my projects, fieldwork, and the things I’m learning along the way.

title: Food Desert Mapping
text:
Overview
In a state with a large population and limited natural resources, ensuring that all residents have access to healthy, affordable food is a persistent challenge. Many communities experience poor health outcomes and resource inaccessibility, especially in areas known as food deserts (places where access to supermarkets and nutritious food options is severely limited). Because food access is a key determinant of overall health, this project explores how food deserts in California intersect with broader geographic and socioeconomic factors. By overlaying spatial data on median income, water scarcity, and community health indicators, the maps in this project highlight how these variables interact and contribute to systemic disparities in access to nutritious food. 


Report and Maps

title: Soil Physical Properties and Water Purity
text:
Overview
How do water bottle companies “mountain purity” images reflect the actual characteristics of mountain filtered ground water as compared to ground water filtered through soil at sea level 
What properties of soil contribute to the purity of water in each location?
Ground water is an extremely important natural resource in California as it makes up 40% of our municipal water supply and up to 60% in drought years. I have noticed throughout my life that bottled water companies like to show images of mountains on their packaging and use words like “natural spring water” in order to get their customers to associate their water with being clean, pure and fresh. In my project I aim to investigate how the properties of mountain soil in Mount Whitney influence the quality of water as compared to the soil near and around UCSB.


Final Presentation
